channel.title: OT/ICS Threat Intel - All Critical Infrastructure
channel.description: Curated OT/ICS advisories, incidents and threat intelligence. Editorial by Mike Holcomb in partnership with UtilSec.

item.title: [Critical] Nidec confirms ransomware at its Taiwan subsidiary as Blackfield demands $2M and claims 2TB of stolen data
item.description: Japanese industrial motor and components maker Nidec has confirmed a ransomware attack on its Taiwanese subsidiary, Nidec Chaun Choung Technology (Nidec CCI), with the Blackfield crew demanding roughly $2 million and claiming it exfiltrated more than two terabytes of corporate data spanning employee, financial, procurement, manufacturing and IT records. To contain the intrusion, Nidec shut down affected servers and network segments and says it is still assessing how production, shipping and other operations were affected. Because Nidec supplies motors, thermal modules and precision components used across manufacturing and critical-infrastructure supply chains, disruption at a plant-adjacent subsidiary ripples downstream to OT operators that depend on those parts. It is also Nidec’s second ransomware event since 2024, underscoring how manufacturers with sprawling global subsidiaries struggle to hold a consistent security baseline. - Nidec disclosure / Blackfield leak site — via BleepingComputer & The Record - Confirmed 22 Jun 2026 — actor claim ~24 Jun 2026

item.title: [High] NATO defense contractor Indra confirms ransomware at one subsidiary as TheGentlemen threatens a data leak
item.description: Spanish defense, aerospace and technology group Indra — a NATO supplier with more than 62,000 staff across 140-plus countries — confirmed a ransomware incident after the fast-rising TheGentlemen crew listed it on a leak site with a countdown deadline. Indra says the event was localized to one non-critical subsidiary environment, did not spread to other group companies and had no effect on service delivery, while an investigation continues. TheGentlemen, a ransomware-as-a-service operation that emerged in late 2024, has claimed more than 300 victims in the first half of 2026 — roughly one in ten of all ransomware claims worldwide, second only to Qilin — and favors organizations with government or defense ties. Even a “contained” hit on a defense-adjacent manufacturer is worth watching for supply-chain and extortion fallout. - Indra statement / TheGentlemen leak site — via Cybernews & SecurityWeek - Listed 30 Jun — confirmed 2 Jul 2026

item.title: [Medium] Mandiant confirms Iran-linked Handala breach of California Water Service stopped short of OT — despite the crew’s disruption boast
item.description: California Water Service (Cal Water), one of the largest U.S. investor-owned water utilities, said Mandiant’s investigation into the breach claimed by Iran-linked group Handala found no evidence of activity in its IT or OT environments. Handala — widely assessed as a front for Iranian operations — had leaked ~5GB of data and asserted it could have disrupted the water supply, implying deep ICS access. Mandiant instead traced the activity to unauthorized access of a small number of user accounts across two third-party service-provider platforms, one active customer’s online account via stolen credentials, and an external GPS-correction site; no billing or payment systems and no control systems were reached. The case is a clean example of separating a threat actor’s psychological claims from verified impact — while underscoring that third-party and customer-facing accounts remain a soft entry point around OT. - Cal Water / Google Mandiant — via SecurityWeek - Investigation findings 25 Jun 2026 — claim 12 Jun 2026

item.title: [Info] Internet-facing ICS plateaus near 170,000 — but Bitsight says the real attack surface is still widening
item.description: Bitsight’s 2026 Global State of ICS/OT Exposure finds the count of internet-facing industrial control systems has flattened at roughly 170,000 devices seen monthly — a number defenders might read as progress. The report’s warning is that the risk profile is expanding anyway: modern controllers increasingly speak general-purpose protocols such as SSH, HTTP and MQTT alongside legacy fieldbus protocols, so each exposed device offers more reachable services and a broader attack surface than the raw count implies. For asset owners the takeaway is that exposure management can’t stop at “is this device on the internet?” — it has to account for which services and protocols each controller exposes and whether any of them are reachable from untrusted networks. - Bitsight — via SecurityWeek - Reported early Jul 2026

item.title: [Info] Dragos launches EmberAI, an OT-tuned AI assistant built on its industrial dataset
item.description: Dragos unveiled EmberAI, an AI capability trained on the company’s large operational-technology cybersecurity dataset and aimed at speeding investigation and triage for OT analysts. The launch lands amid a broader debate over AI’s two-edged role in industrial security — the same week’s reporting includes threat actors leaning on general-purpose AI to navigate toward OT assets. The practical question for asset owners is where domain-specific, OT-context AI genuinely reduces analyst workload and mean-time-to-understand versus adding another tool to validate. Treat it as a signal that the major OT platforms are racing to embed AI, and evaluate any such assistant on how well it respects OT data-handling, segmentation and human-in-the-loop control rather than on demo polish. - Dragos — via SecurityWeek - Announced 23 Jun 2026

item.title: [Critical] Progress Kemp LoadMaster: pre-auth RCE (CVSS 9.6) now under active exploitation at the network edge
item.description: A critical OS command-injection flaw, CVE-2026-8037 (CVSS 9.6), lets an unauthenticated remote attacker run arbitrary commands on Progress Kemp LoadMaster load balancers by reaching the appliance’s API. Because LoadMaster typically sits at the outer edge of enterprise and IT/OT boundary networks, a successful hit hands attackers a direct, credential-free foothold into the environment. Progress disclosed the bug on 4 June and shipped a fix; functional proof-of-concept code appeared on 29 June, and security firms and H-ISAC reported exploitation attempts beginning the same day. This is exactly the kind of edge-appliance flaw OT teams should treat as an IT/OT-boundary emergency, since a compromised balancer can become a pivot toward control networks and remote-access paths. - CVE-2026-8037 - Disclosed 4 Jun — exploitation from 29 Jun 2026

item.title: [Critical] First in-the-wild exploitation of PTC Windchill RCE lands it in CISA’s KEV catalog
item.description: CISA added CVE-2026-12569, a remote code-execution flaw in PTC Windchill, to its Known Exploited Vulnerabilities catalog after the first confirmed in-the-wild exploitation of the bug. Windchill is a widely used product-lifecycle-management platform that holds engineering designs, bills of material and manufacturing data — the “crown jewels” that sit adjacent to, and feed, OT production. Exploitation of a PLM system can expose intellectual property and give attackers a route toward manufacturing operations and supply-chain partners. Manufacturers running Windchill should treat the KEV listing as a hard prompt to patch on the KEV timeline and to check exposure of the application to untrusted networks. - CVE-2026-12569 - Added to KEV 26 Jun 2026

item.title: [High] Lantronix serial-to-IP converter flaw exploited in attacks, added to CISA KEV
item.description: Attackers are now exploiting CVE-2025-67038, a code-injection flaw in Lantronix serial-to-IP converters, and CISA has added it to the KEV catalog — exploitation that followed earlier warnings about targeting of exposed OT devices. Serial-to-IP gateways are the connective tissue that brings legacy serial field equipment onto IP networks, so a compromised converter can give an attacker a bridge between reachable networks and older, unauthenticated control devices behind it. The flaw was among those surfaced by the BRIDGE:BREAK research effort disclosed earlier in 2026. Asset owners should locate these converters, remove internet exposure, and apply Lantronix’s fixes. - CVE-2025-67038 - Exploitation reported 25 Jun 2026

item.title: [High] Rockwell Automation patches vulnerabilities across Logix, RSLinx and FactoryTalk
item.description: Rockwell Automation issued fixes for security flaws spanning its Logix and CompactLogix controllers, Flex I/O, RSLinx communications software and FactoryTalk products — core building blocks of discrete and process automation in many plants. No confirmed in-the-wild exploitation was reported alongside the advisories, but the breadth of affected products means most Rockwell-heavy environments will have something to remediate. Given that Iran-linked activity has specifically targeted internet-exposed Rockwell/Allen-Bradley PLCs this year, keeping this ecosystem patched and off the public internet is doubly important. Asset owners should map affected versions against their installed base and schedule updates through normal OT change control. - Rockwell Automation PSIRT — via SecurityWeek - Advisories 17 Jun 2026

item.title: [High] Daktronics controller flaws could let attackers hijack highway signs and billboards
item.description: CISA advisory ICSA-26-176-04 covers three vulnerabilities in Daktronics controller firmware (VFC-DMP-5000, DMP-5000 and DMP-8000) that, chained, could give a remote attacker root-level control over what the signs display — via path traversal, an unrestricted file upload and a weak default administrative account. While tampering with roadway signs and billboards is more disruption-and-disinformation than process safety, spoofed traffic messages carry real public-safety and public-trust consequences, and root on an internet-exposed controller is a serious foothold. Daktronics released patched firmware in March; the risk is concentrated in units left exposed with default credentials. Transportation and outdoor-display operators should update firmware and replace default passwords. - CVE-2026-28701 - CISA advisory 25 Jun 2026

item.title: [Medium] Schneider Easergy MiCOM Px40 protection relays ship with hard-coded SNMP credentials
item.description: CISA advisory ICSA-26-190-03, published 9 July, flags CVE-2026-4832 (CVSS 5.3) in Schneider Electric’s Easergy MiCOM Px40 protection relays — hard-coded credentials in the relay’s SNMP implementation that let an unauthenticated attacker who can reach the SNMP port read sensitive device information. Protection relays are safety-critical grid devices, so even an information-disclosure flaw is worth closing: leaked device data can aid targeting and reconnaissance of transmission and distribution assets. Affected models include P14x, P24x and P341 below specified firmware levels. This landed alongside CISA advisories for Schneider PowerChute Serial Shutdown and OpenPLC v3 in the same 9 July batch. - CVE-2026-4832 - CISA advisory 9 Jul 2026

item.title: [Policy] Australia’s enhanced CIRMP rules expand critical-infrastructure obligations to AI, legacy and supply-chain risk
item.description: Australia’s Cyber and Infrastructure Security Centre unveiled enhanced Critical Infrastructure Risk Management Program (CIRMP) rules that broaden what responsible entities must address — explicitly folding in AI-related risk, aging legacy systems, supply-chain dependencies and insider threats. For operators inside or trading with Australia’s SOCI regime, it is a prompt to revisit risk-management programs so these categories are documented and managed, not assumed. More broadly it reflects a global regulatory drift toward naming specific systemic risks — legacy OT, third parties, AI — rather than relying on generic “reasonable steps” language, and gives asset owners elsewhere a useful checklist for their own OT risk programs. - Australia CISC — via Industrial Cyber - Announced 18 Jun 2026

item.title: [Policy] NIST publishes SP-1339 OT Backup Quick Start Guide to speed industrial recovery
item.description: NIST released SP-1339, an OT Backup Quick Start Guide aimed at helping industrial operators build backup and recovery practices tuned to control-system realities — where restoration can involve physical processes, safety interlocks and firmware provenance, not just re-imaging a server. It is practical, actionable guidance rather than a mandate, and it dovetails with the recurring incident-response lesson that OT recovery fails when backups aren’t OT-aware or offline-tested. Asset owners can use it as a template to validate that controller configurations, logic and engineering-workstation images are captured, protected and recoverable — and to rehearse recovery before an incident forces the issue. - NIST — via Industrial Cyber - Released 19 Jun 2026
